takeaways: Senior housing enters Q2 2026 with strong tailwinds: Harrison Street projects 3–6% rental rate growth, demand is rising, and development pipelines remain historically thin — a favorable supply/demand setup for operators like Lloyd Jones.

CRE capital markets are reopening: Colliers forecasts a 15–20% jump in 2026 sales activity as the 10-Year Treasury stabilizes around 4.00–4.25% and conventional commercial mortgage rates settle between 5.08% and 8.75%.

AI is moving from experiment to execution across real estate — agentic AI is automating lease drafting, tenant relationship management, and portfolio decisions, giving early adopters material cost and operational advantages.

items.headline: Harrison Street projects 3–6% senior housing rental growth in 2026
items.summary: Harrison Street expects senior housing fundamentals to remain strong in 2026, with rental rate growth trending between 3% and 6%. Active adult communities fall at the lower end, while higher-acuity units in supply-constrained markets push toward the upper end — a direct read-through for Lloyd Jones' IL/AL mix and market selection.

items.headline: U.S. News names 1,614 communities to 2026 Best Senior Living ratings
items.summary: U.S. News & World Report released its 2026 Best Senior Living ratings on April 14, assessing more than 3,000 communities and naming 1,614 as 'best.' Now in its fifth year, the list is a key marketing and lead-gen signal for IL, AL, memory care, and CCRC operators competing for move-ins.

items.headline: Senior living costs keep climbing — pricing power holds into 2026
items.summary: Median senior living costs rose again across independent living, assisted living, and memory care from 2024 to 2025, extending a multiyear trend. Continued pricing power supports NOI expansion for operators but raises affordability and middle-market risk that Lloyd Jones should monitor.

items.headline: St. John's expands capacity as demand for senior living surges
items.summary: St. John's Living (April 13) outlined expansion plans to meet accelerating demand for senior housing — echoing sector-wide commentary that demand from older adults continues to rise while new development remains constrained. Reinforces the case for acquiring or repositioning existing assets over ground-up builds.

items.headline: Colliers forecasts 15–20% rise in 2026 CRE sales activity
items.summary: Colliers projects a 15–20% increase in commercial real estate sales activity for 2026 as institutional and cross-border capital returns. Capital is flowing again, interest rates are moving lower, and leasing fundamentals are stabilizing — a constructive backdrop for senior housing dispositions and acquisitions.

items.headline: 10-Year Treasury stabilizes at 4.00–4.25%, anchoring CRE loan pricing
items.summary: As of April 11, 2026, commercial loan rates range from 5.08% to 12.75%, with conventional CRE mortgages pricing between 5.08% and 8.75%. Most fixed-rate CRE loans are benchmarked off the 10-Year Treasury, which has held at 4.00–4.25% since mid-2025 — giving sponsors a clearer basis for underwriting refis and new acquisitions.

items.headline: Debt markets flush with dry powder; inflation forecast near 3%
items.summary: Deloitte and others note debt markets should remain healthy in 2026 with ample liquidity from public and private investors. Stabilizing inflation near 3% and more flexible monetary policy are supporting access to CRE debt capital, particularly for beds, sheds, and needs-based assets like senior housing.

items.headline: CBRE 2026 outlook: capital markets recovery broadens beyond multifamily
items.summary: CBRE's 2026 U.S. Real Estate Market Outlook points to a broader capital markets recovery, with transaction volume picking up across sectors as price discovery improves and bid-ask spreads compress. Expect more competition for stabilized senior housing assets in core and core-plus sub-markets.

items.headline: Agentic AI takes over leasing, procurement, and portfolio workflows
items.summary: ICSC reports agentic AI is emerging as the next phase of proptech — automating procurement, pricing, inventory, and event programming. Early adopters in retail and real estate are gaining significant operational and cost advantages as AI agents move beyond content generation into autonomous execution.

items.headline: Real estate owners prioritize tenant CRM, lease drafting, portfolio AI
items.summary: According to ICSC, tenant relationship management, lease drafting, and portfolio management are the top AI use cases real estate owners are actively vetting in 2026. Less tolerance for isolated tools — the 2026 mandate is making the existing tech stack work end-to-end.

items.headline: Proptech boss: 2026 is the year AI moves from experimentation to delivery
items.summary: Industry leaders say 2026 marks the inflection point where AI in proptech shifts from pilots to production — becoming a practical driver of efficiency and NOI rather than an experiment. Real estate, construction, and infrastructure firms are streamlining ops and decision-making with deployed solutions.

items.headline: AI 'digital twin' cities predict retailer performance by location
items.summary: Some real estate companies are experimenting with AI models that create mirror-image digital twins of cities — matching demographics and spending patterns to forecast sales volumes and retailer performance when considering relocation. Similar approaches could sharpen senior housing site selection and unit-mix planning.

items.headline: Microsoft: leaders scale AI by anchoring to outcomes, not tools
items.summary: Microsoft's April 1 business insights highlight that firms accelerating AI adoption are tying deployment to specific business outcomes — faster decision-making, better client experience — rather than rolling out tools ad hoc. One professional services firm cut cycle times by redesigning workflows end-to-end.

items.headline: DDI: 71% of leaders report increased stress, 40% eye the exit
items.summary: DDI's Global Leadership Forecast 2025 finds 71% of leaders are under increased stress and 40% are considering leaving their jobs. For senior housing operators — where ED and nursing leadership turnover directly hits occupancy and margins — retention programs are now a board-level issue.

items.headline: Human + AI leadership is the dominant 2026 management trend
items.summary: Top 2026 leadership trends include human + AI leadership, flatter organizational structures, rising 'quiet cracking' burnout, stalled mobility from 'job hugging,' and renewed focus on human-centered skills. AI handles technical tasks; emotional intelligence and creativity become the differentiators.

items.headline: Strategic problem-solving now a top hiring priority for 90% of leaders
items.summary: Industry research cited by Aspen Institute finds 90% of business leaders now rank strategic problem-solving as a top priority for hiring and growth. Expect increased competition for mid-level strategy talent — a signal to Lloyd Jones to double down on internal leadership development.

items.headline: Nervous-system regulation replaces hustle as 2026's wellness headline
items.summary: With 45% of adults reporting burnout, 2026 wellness is pivoting to nervous-system regulation — 'presence is replacing productivity.' Operators of senior communities can lean into the trend through stress-reduction programming, mindfulness spaces, and calming design that differentiates IL/AL offerings.

items.headline: Longevity goes mainstream: strength, sleep, nutrition, community
items.summary: Longevity has shifted from niche biohacking to a comprehensive mainstream lifestyle — blending metabolic health, functional nutrition, strength training, restorative sleep, and community connection. A direct opportunity for senior living to package programming around evidence-based longevity pillars.

items.headline: GWI releases 2026 workplace wellbeing initiative trends
items.summary: The Global Wellness Institute's April 7 release highlights a shift toward mental health support, flexible work, preventive care, and behavior-change programs — with employers investing in long-term wellbeing over short-term perks. Relevant for Lloyd Jones' corporate HR and on-site community staff retention.

items.headline: 'Movement snacks' emerge as the anti-sedentary productivity hack
items.summary: Wellness publications are promoting 'movement snacks' — short bursts of stretching or mobility scattered through the day — to counter prolonged sitting, protect joints, and keep metabolism active. Easy-to-deploy programming for IL/AL activities calendars and corporate wellness alike.
